entries.name: Mataranka Thermal Pool
entries.tag: Elsey National Park, Northern Territory, Australia
entries.desc: A clear, warm spring-fed pool ringed by palms and paperbark forest in the Northern Territory's Elsey National Park. It is a well-known rest stop for travellers crossing the Australian outback.

photo.alt: Swimmers in the clear, palm-fringed Mataranka Thermal Pool
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mataranka-thermal-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Mataranka Thermal Springs.jpg" by Kendra Baker · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mataranka_Thermal_Springs.jpg -->

entries.name: Peninsula Hot Springs
entries.desc: Australia's first natural hot springs bathing centre, on the Mornington Peninsula south of Melbourne. Its spread-out grounds include a hilltop pool with panoramic views over the surrounding countryside.

photo.alt: Cape Schanck's coastal cliffs and sea stack on the Mornington Peninsula
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cape-schanck-mornington-peninsula.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Cape Schanck JJBM 1.jpg" by Juan Jose Basagoiti Mancera · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cape_Schanck_JJBM_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Aqua Dome
entries.desc: A modern thermal spa in Austria's Ötztal valley, known for its trio of elevated saucer-shaped outdoor pools framed by the surrounding Tyrolean Alps. Its water is drawn from a deep sulphurous spring.

photo.alt: Aqua Dome's elevated saucer-shaped outdoor pools framed by the Tyrolean Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: aqua-dome-langenfeld-pools.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 DE · "AQUA DOME Außen Schalen 2.jpg" by VAMED AG · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:AQUA_DOME_Au%C3%9Fen_Schalen_2.jpg -->

entries.name: Termas de Polques
entries.desc: A small thermal pool high on the Bolivian altiplano within the Eduardo Avaroa reserve, a common stop on Uyuni salt-flat tours. It sits beside a lagoon at over 4,000 metres amid stark desert scenery.

photo.alt: Bathers in the Termas de Polques thermal pool on Bolivia's altiplano
photo.credit: <!-- photo: termas-de-polques-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Aguas termales, Laguna Salada, Bolivia, 2016-02-02, DD 110.JPG" by Diego Delso · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aguas_termales,_Laguna_Salada,_Bolivia,_2016-02-02,_DD_110.JPG -->

entries.name: Lençóis Thermal Springs
entries.desc: Springs in Caldas Novas, a Brazilian town sitting atop one of the world's largest hydrothermal aquifers. The naturally warm groundwater feeds numerous resort water parks in the region.

photo.alt: Wave pool at a hot-spring resort in the Caldas Novas/Rio Quente thermal region
photo.credit: <!-- photo: rio-quente-resort-wave-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Piscinas de ondas.jpg" by Eric Gandolph · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Piscinas_de_ondas.jpg -->

entries.name: Banff Upper Hot Springs
entries.desc: The highest hot springs in Canada, in Banff National Park in the Canadian Rockies. Bathers soak in the historic mineral pool while looking out toward Mount Rundle and the surrounding peaks.

photo.alt: Bathers in the historic Banff Upper Hot Springs pool with mountain views
photo.credit: <!-- photo: banff-upper-hot-springs-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Banff Upper Hot Springs.JPG" by Vigorous action · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Banff_Upper_Hot_Springs.JPG -->

entries.name: Liard River Hot Springs
entries.desc: The second-largest hot springs in Canada, in a provincial park along the Alaska Highway in northern British Columbia. Warm water sustains a lush boreal spring ecosystem and boardwalk-accessed pools.

photo.alt: Boardwalk and steaming pool at Liard River Hot Springs
photo.credit: <!-- photo: liard-river-hot-springs-boardwalk.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Alpha pool at Liard River Hotsprings, British Columbia.JPG" by Lee Tengum · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Alpha_pool_at_Liard_River_Hotsprings,_British_Columbia.JPG -->

entries.name: Termas de Chillán
entries.desc: A mountain resort in the Chilean Andes combining a ski area with thermal pools fed by the active Nevados de Chillán volcano, letting visitors ski and soak in the same volcanic setting.

photo.alt: Snow-covered ski slopes and lodge at Termas de Chillán
photo.credit: <!-- photo: termas-de-chillan-ski-resort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Termas de Chillán, Chile.jpg" by Teresa Fischer (Marca Chile) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Termas_de_Chill%C3%A1n,_Chile.jpg -->

entries.name: Termas de Puritama
entries.desc: A chain of terraced thermal pools in a desert canyon near San Pedro de Atacama, fed by a warm stream high on the Andean altiplano. The pools sit amid reeds in an otherwise arid landscape.

photo.alt: Terraced thermal pools in the Puritama canyon, Atacama Desert
photo.credit: <!-- photo: puritama-canyon-pools.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Termas de Puritama, Desierto de Atacama. Chile.jpg" by Luciajimena1990 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Termas_de_Puritama,_Desierto_de_Atacama._Chile.jpg -->

entries.name: Termas Geométricas
entries.desc: A designed hot spring complex in Chile's southern Andes, where slate pools are linked by red wooden walkways threading up a lush rainforest canyon past waterfalls and steaming streams.

photo.alt: Slate pools and red wooden walkways at Termas Geométricas, Coñaripe
photo.credit: <!-- photo: termas-geometricas-walkways.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Termas Geometricas, Coñaripe - Flickr - rgamper.jpg" by Ralf Christian Gamper · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Termas_Geometricas,_Co%C3%B1aripe_-_Flickr_-_rgamper.jpg -->

entries.name: Huangshan Hot Springs
entries.desc: Springs at the foot of the Yellow Mountains, a UNESCO-listed range in Anhui renowned for granite peaks and sea-of-clouds views. The waters have been used for bathing since the Tang Dynasty.

photo.alt: Yellow Mountains (Huangshan) granite peaks, Anhui, China
photo.credit: <!-- photo: huangshan-yellow-mountains.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "China Anhui Huang Shan scenic view 1.JPG" by Boule02de · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:China_Anhui_Huang_Shan_scenic_view_1.JPG -->

entries.name: Yangpachen Hot Springs
entries.desc: Among the highest hot springs in the world, on the Tibetan Plateau northwest of Lhasa at over 4,000 metres. The geothermal field also powers one of Tibet's main geothermal energy plants.

photo.alt: Yangpachen Valley on the Tibetan Plateau near Lhasa
photo.credit: <!-- photo: yangpachen-valley.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "YangpachenValley.jpg" by McKay Savage · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:YangpachenValley.jpg -->

entries.name: Tabacón Thermal Resort
entries.desc: A resort near the Arenal Volcano in Costa Rica, where naturally heated thermal water flows through landscaped tropical gardens and pools. The springs are warmed by the volcano's geothermal system.

photo.alt: Tabacón Hot Springs near Arenal Volcano, Costa Rica
photo.credit: <!-- photo: tabacon-resort-costa-rica.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Tabacón Hot Springs, Costa Rica.jpg" by the U.S. Central Intelligence Agency · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tabac%C3%B3n_Hot_Springs,_Costa_Rica.jpg -->

entries.name: Karlovy Vary Hot Springs
entries.desc: A historic spa town founded in the 14th century, laced with colonnades where visitors drink from more than a dozen mineral springs. Its hottest geyser, the Vřídlo, jets water high into the air.

photo.alt: The spa colonnade in Karlovy Vary, Czech Republic
photo.credit: <!-- photo: karlovy-vary-colonnade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "КВ-колонада.jpg" by ГП · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:%D0%9A%D0%92-%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B0.jpg -->

entries.name: Dominica's Hot Springs
entries.desc: A cluster of sulphur springs and mud pools in the village of Wotten Waven on the volcanic island of Dominica. Set in rainforest, they lie near the Boiling Lake and Morne Trois Pitons National Park.

photo.alt: Fumaroles in the Valley of Desolation, Dominica
photo.credit: <!-- photo: valley-of-desolation-dominica.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Furmaholes, Valley of Desolation, Dominica.jpg" by XeresNelro · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Furmaholes,_Valley_of_Desolation,_Dominica.jpg -->

entries.name: Termas de Papallacta
entries.desc: High-altitude thermal pools in the Andes east of Quito, near the entrance to Cayambe Coca reserve. On clear days bathers can see surrounding volcanoes from the steaming mountain pools.

photo.alt: Thermal springs at Papallacta in the Ecuadorian Andes
photo.credit: <!-- photo: papallacta-thermal-springs.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0/4.0 · "B02 Ecuador 021 Thermalquellen Papallacta - 1985.jpg" by Hubertl · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:B02_Ecuador_021_Thermalquellen_Papallacta_-_1985.jpg -->

entries.name: Dallol Hydrothermal Fields
entries.desc: Otherworldly acid springs and salt formations in Ethiopia's Danakil Depression, one of the hottest and lowest places on Earth. The vivid yellow and green terraces are strictly for viewing on guided trips.

photo.alt: Vivid orange and yellow acid-terrace formations at Dallol in the Danakil Depression
photo.credit: <!-- photo: dallol-hydrothermal-fields.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Dallol-Ethiopie (19).jpg" by Ji-Elle · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Dallol-Ethiopie_(19).jpg -->

entries.name: Friedrichsbad & Caracalla Spa
entries.desc: Two connected bathhouses in a spa town whose thermal waters were tapped by the Romans. The 19th-century Friedrichsbad offers a traditional Roman-Irish ritual, while the Caracalla is a modern pool complex.

photo.alt: Exterior facade of the Friedrichsbad bathhouse in Baden-Baden, Germany
photo.credit: <!-- photo: friedrichsbad-baden-baden.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Baden-Baden-Friedrichsbad-01-Suedost-gje.jpg" by Gerd Eichmann · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Baden-Baden-Friedrichsbad-01-Suedost-gje.jpg -->

meta: UNESCO Great Spa Towns of Europe

entries.name: Pozar Thermal Baths
entries.desc: Natural thermal springs in northern Greece, where warm mineral water flows through a forested gorge into open-air pools and cascades. The setting combines bathing with waterfalls and cave passages.

photo.alt: Bathers in an open-air thermal pool beneath waterfalls at Pozar
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pozar-thermal-baths.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Loutra Pozar 8265323.JPG" by C messier · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Loutra_Pozar_8265323.JPG -->

entries.name: Gellért Thermal Bath
entries.desc: An Art Nouveau bathhouse at the foot of Gellért Hill, celebrated for its ornate mosaic tiling, stained glass, and columned indoor pool. It draws on spring water known since the Middle Ages.

photo.alt: Art Nouveau facade of the Gellért Baths and Hotel at the foot of Gellért Hill
photo.credit: <!-- photo: gellert-baths-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "20190502 Hotel Gellért w Budapeszcie 1706 2154 DxO.jpg" by Jakub Hałun · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:20190502_Hotel_Gell%C3%A9rt_w_Budapeszcie_1706_2154_DxO.jpg -->

entries.name: Rudas Thermal Bath
entries.desc: A Turkish bath dating to the Ottoman occupation of the 1550s, with an octagonal pool beneath a domed roof pierced by coloured skylights. A modern rooftop pool overlooks the Danube.

photo.alt: Rudas Baths along the Danube riverbank beneath Gellért Hill
photo.credit: <!-- photo: rudas-baths-exterior.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Budapest, Gellért-hegy, Rudas fürdő.jpg" by Christo · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Budapest,_Gell%C3%A9rt-hegy,_Rudas_f%C3%BCrd%C5%91.jpg -->

entries.name: Széchenyi Thermal Bath
entries.desc: Europe's largest medicinal bath complex, housed in a grand neo-Baroque palace in Budapest's City Park. Its outdoor pools, fed by deep thermal wells, stay warm even in winter.

photo.alt: Neo-baroque yellow palace and outdoor thermal pools of Széchenyi Bath in City Park
photo.credit: <!-- photo: szechenyi-baths.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Budapest Széchenyi Baths R01.jpg" by Marc Ryckaert · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Budapest_Sz%C3%A9chenyi_Baths_R01.jpg -->

entries.name: Blue Lagoon
entries.desc: A geothermal spa set in a lava field on the Reykjanes Peninsula, fed by mineral-rich seawater from a nearby power plant. Its milky-blue silica waters are among Iceland's most-visited attractions.

photo.alt: Milky-blue silica waters of the Blue Lagoon with the geothermal power plant behind
photo.credit: <!-- photo: blue-lagoon-iceland.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg" by McKay Savage · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Iceland_-_Blue_Lagoon_09_(6571266721).jpg -->

entries.name: Landmannalaugar
entries.desc: A natural hot spring in Iceland's remote highlands, ringed by rhyolite mountains in vivid mineral hues. It marks the start of the Laugavegur trekking route and is reachable only in summer.

photo.alt: Rhyolite mountains in vivid mineral hues in the Landmannalaugar highlands
photo.credit: <!-- photo: landmannalaugar-mountains.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Landmannalaugar Iceland 2005 2.JPG" by Chris 73 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Landmannalaugar_Iceland_2005_2.JPG -->

entries.name: Laugarvatn Fontana
entries.desc: A geothermal bath complex on the Golden Circle beside Lake Laugarvatn, built over natural steam vents. Rye bread is traditionally baked in the hot ground here and served to visitors.

photo.alt: Fontana geothermal spa buildings on the shore of Lake Laugarvatn, Iceland
photo.credit: <!-- photo: laugarvatn-fontana.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Fontana spa.jpg" by Reykholt · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fontana_spa.jpg -->

entries.name: Mývatn Nature Baths
entries.desc: A geothermal lagoon in northern Iceland near Lake Mývatn, fed by alkaline mineral water drawn from deep boreholes. It offers a quieter alternative to the Blue Lagoon with volcanic surroundings.

photo.alt: Milky-blue geothermal lagoon at Mývatn Nature Baths surrounded by volcanic terrain, North Iceland
photo.credit: <!-- photo: myvatn-nature-baths.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Myvatn Nature Baths by Bruce McAdam.jpg" by Bruce McAdam · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Myvatn_Nature_Baths_by_Bruce_McAdam.jpg -->

entries.name: Secret Lagoon (Gamla Laugin)
entries.desc: Iceland's oldest swimming pool, built in 1891 in the village of Flúðir along the Golden Circle. Natural steam vents and a small geyser surround its warm geothermal waters.

photo.alt: Houses in the village of Flúðir in South Iceland, home to the Secret Lagoon
photo.credit: <!-- photo: fludir-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Houses of fludir.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Houses_of_fludir.jpg -->

entries.name: Seljavallalaug
entries.desc: One of Iceland's oldest pools, built into a hillside in 1923 and reached by a short walk through a mountain valley. Its water is cooler than most spas and free to visit.

photo.alt: Seljavallalaug geothermal pool set in a hillside valley in South Iceland
photo.credit: <!-- photo: seljavallalaug-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Seljavallalaug hills.JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Seljavallalaug_hills.JPG -->

entries.name: Sky Lagoon
entries.desc: A clifftop geothermal lagoon on the edge of Reykjavik with an infinity edge facing the North Atlantic. It features a multi-step bathing ritual inspired by Icelandic tradition.

photo.alt: Exterior of Sky Lagoon with turf-roofed building on the edge of Reykjavik
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sky-lagoon-exterior.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Sky Lagoon Exterior.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sky_Lagoon_Exterior.jpg -->

entries.name: Manikaran Hot Springs
entries.desc: Sacred springs in the Parvati Valley of the Himalayas, revered by both Hindus and Sikhs and flanked by a temple and gurudwara. The water is hot enough to cook rice and lentils for pilgrims.

photo.alt: Gurudwara and river at Manikaran in the Parvati Valley, Himachal Pradesh
photo.credit: <!-- photo: manikaran-gurudwara.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Manikaran Sahib Gurudwara.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Manikaran_Sahib_Gurudwara.jpg -->

entries.name: Banjar Hot Springs
entries.desc: Sulphur hot springs in northern Bali, where warm water pours from carved stone dragon spouts into tiered bathing pools set among tropical gardens. They are a longtime local bathing site.

photo.alt: Stone dragon-spout hot spring pools at Air Panas Banjar in northern Bali
photo.credit: <!-- photo: banjar-hot-springs.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Thermes d'Air Panas Banjar.JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Thermes_d'Air_Panas_Banjar.JPG -->

entries.name: Bagno Vignoni
entries.desc: A Tuscan village unique for its central square, which is occupied by a large stone thermal pool rather than a piazza. Used since Roman and Renaissance times, the steaming basin is now for viewing only.

photo.alt: Bagno Vignoni's central piazza occupied by a large stone thermal pool, Val d'Orcia
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bagno-vignoni-piazza.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "Bagno Vignoni.jpg" by Petitverdot · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bagno_Vignoni.jpg -->

entries.name: Saturnia Hot Springs
entries.desc: Free-flowing sulphurous springs in southern Tuscany, best known for the Cascate del Mulino, where warm water spills over travertine terraces into a series of natural stone pools open around the clock.

photo.alt: Cascate del Mulino travertine terraces at Saturnia
photo.credit: <!-- photo: saturnia-cascate-del-mulino.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "Saturnia Cascate del Mulino.jpg" by Markus Bernet · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Saturnia_Cascate_del_Mulino.jpg -->

entries.name: Beppu Onsen
entries.desc: One of Japan's most productive hot spring towns, discharging vast volumes of geothermal water from thousands of vents. Its "Hells" are vividly coloured boiling ponds meant for viewing rather than bathing.

photo.alt: Chinoike Jigoku (Blood Pond Hell), one of Beppu's boiling ponds
photo.credit: <!-- photo: beppu-chinoike-jigoku.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "Beppu Chinoike-jigoku01n4272.jpg" by 663highland · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Beppu_Chinoike-jigoku01n4272.jpg -->

entries.name: Dōgo Onsen
entries.desc: Among the oldest hot springs in Japan, referenced in ancient chronicles. Its landmark wooden bathhouse in Matsuyama dates to 1894 and is said to have inspired the bathhouse in the film Spirited Away.

photo.alt: Dōgo Onsen Honkan bathhouse illuminated at night
photo.credit: <!-- photo: dogo-onsen-honkan.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Dogo onsen honkan long exposure.jpg" by Japanexperterna.se · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Dogo_onsen_honkan_long_exposure.jpg -->

entries.name: Hakone Onsen
entries.desc: A mountain resort area near Tokyo dotted with hot spring inns, long favoured as a weekend retreat. On clear days its baths and lakeside look out toward Mount Fuji.

photo.alt: Lake Ashi with Mount Fuji in the distance, Hakone
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hakone-lake-ashi-mount-fuji.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Mount Fuji @ Lake Ashi @ Hakone (13776927014).jpg" by Guilhem Vellut · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mount_Fuji_@_Lake_Ashi_@_Hakone_(13776927014).jpg -->

entries.name: Ibusuki Sand Baths
entries.desc: A coastal town in Kagoshima famous for a rare form of bathing in which visitors are buried up to the neck in naturally hot volcanic sand warmed by geothermal water along the shore.

photo.alt: Steam rising from the volcanic sand beach at Ibusuki
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ibusuki-volcanic-sand-beach.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Hot volcanic sands- hot steam - panoramio.jpg" by BirdsEyeLV · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hot_volcanic_sands-_hot_steam_-_panoramio.jpg -->

entries.name: Jigokudani Monkey Park
entries.desc: A valley in the Nagano mountains famous for wild Japanese macaques that bathe in a natural hot spring pool, especially in winter snow. The pool is for the monkeys; visitors watch from the banks.

photo.alt: Japanese macaques bathing in the snow monkey hot spring pool
photo.credit: <!-- photo: jigokudani-snow-monkeys.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Snow Monkeys, Nagano, Japan.JPG" by Yblieb · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Snow_Monkeys,_Nagano,_Japan.JPG -->

entries.name: Kinosaki Onsen
entries.desc: A willow-lined hot spring town on the Sea of Japan coast, built around seven public bathhouses. Guests stroll between them in traditional robes and wooden sandals, a custom central to its charm.

photo.alt: Willow-lined canal through Kinosaki Onsen
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kinosaki-onsen-canal.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "Kinosaki onsen02 1920.jpg" by 663highland · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kinosaki_onsen02_1920.jpg -->

entries.name: Kurokawa Onsen
entries.desc: A secluded hot spring town on Kyushu, set in a wooded river gorge and known for preserving a rustic, low-rise character. A single pass lets visitors sample outdoor baths across several inns.

photo.alt: Kurokawa Onsen hot spring town in its wooded river gorge
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kurokawa-onsen-gorge.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Kurokawa onsen 001.JPG" by Yosemite~commonswiki · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kurokawa_onsen_001.JPG -->

entries.name: Kusatsu Onsen
entries.desc: A leading hot spring town in Gunma, prized for its highly acidic sulphur water. At its centre the Yubatake field channels and cools the steaming water through long wooden troughs.

photo.alt: Yubatake hot spring field with wooden cooling troughs, Kusatsu Onsen, Japan
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kusatsu-onsen-yubatake.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Kusatsu Yubatake 03.JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kusatsu_Yubatake_03.JPG -->

entries.name: Noboribetsu Onsen
entries.desc: Hokkaido's best-known hot spring resort, supplied by the steaming Jigokudani or "Hell Valley," a volcanic crater above the town. Its waters span an unusually wide range of mineral types.

photo.alt: Jigokudani (Hell Valley) volcanic crater above Noboribetsu Onsen, Hokkaido
photo.credit: <!-- photo: noboribetsu-jigokudani.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Noboribetsu-jigokudani.JPG" by Prosperosity · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Noboribetsu-jigokudani.JPG -->

entries.name: Lake Bogoria Hot Springs
entries.desc: Geysers and boiling springs along the shore of Lake Bogoria in Kenya's Rift Valley. The alkaline lake draws vast flocks of lesser flamingos; the springs themselves are for viewing only.

photo.alt: Geyser steaming along the shore of Lake Bogoria, Kenya
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lake-bogoria-geyser.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "200812 kenya-107 (3213939312).jpg" by Franco Pecchio · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:200812_kenya-107_(3213939312).jpg -->

entries.name: Hierve el Agua
entries.desc: A set of mineral springs in Oaxaca whose carbonate-rich water has formed dramatic "petrified waterfalls" of rock down a cliff. Clifftop pools offer swimming and sweeping valley views.

photo.alt: Petrified waterfall rock formation at Hierve el Agua, Oaxaca
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hierve-el-agua-petrified-waterfall.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Cascada petrificada de Hierve el Agua en Oaxaca.jpg" by Holamanueldiaz2 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cascada_petrificada_de_Hierve_el_Agua_en_Oaxaca.jpg -->

entries.name: Hanmer Springs
entries.desc: An alpine spa village in the mountains of Canterbury on New Zealand's South Island, built around thermal pools first developed in the late 1800s. It is a popular year-round mountain getaway.

photo.alt: Hanmer Springs alpine village seen from Conical Hill, Canterbury, New Zealand
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hanmer-springs-alpine-village.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Hanmer Springs Alpine Village view from Conical Hill- North Canterbury- South Island- New Zealand.jpg" by FutureKiwi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hanmer_Springs_Alpine_Village_view_from_Conical_Hill-_North_Canterbury-_South_Island-_New_Zealand.jpg -->

entries.name: Hot Water Beach
entries.desc: A beach on the Coromandel Peninsula where geothermal water rises just beneath the sand. Around low tide, visitors dig their own pools in the beach and soak in the warm seep.

photo.alt: Hot Water Beach on the Coromandel Peninsula, New Zealand
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hot-water-beach-coromandel.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Hot Water Beach on Coromandel Peninsula 02.jpg" by Krzysztof Golik · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hot_Water_Beach_on_Coromandel_Peninsula_02.jpg -->

entries.name: Kerosene Creek
entries.desc: A free geothermal stream south of Rotorua running through bush, with a small warm waterfall and pool popular for soaking. It is undeveloped, with no facilities or supervision.

photo.alt: Kerosene Creek geothermal waterfall and pool near Rotorua
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kerosene-creek-geothermal-waterfall.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Kerosene Creek, Geothermal Hot Waterfall (31904686743).jpg" by Brendan Purdy · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kerosene_Creek,_Geothermal_Hot_Waterfall_(31904686743).jpg -->

entries.name: Polynesian Spa
entries.desc: A geothermal bathing complex on the shore of Lake Rotorua, drawing on two mineral springs of differing character. Rotorua has long been a centre of Maori thermal culture and volcanic activity.

photo.alt: Lake Rotorua, on whose shore the Polynesian Spa sits, New Zealand
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lake-rotorua.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Lake Rotorua in Rotorua 02.jpg" by Krzysztof Golik · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lake_Rotorua_in_Rotorua_02.jpg -->

entries.name: Wai-O-Tapu
entries.desc: A geothermal wonderland near Rotorua known for brightly coloured pools, including the Champagne Pool and the neon-green Devil's Bath. Its features are for viewing, not bathing.

photo.alt: The turquoise and orange-rimmed Champagne Pool at Wai-O-Tapu geothermal park
photo.credit: <!-- photo: champagne-pool-wai-o-tapu.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Champagne Pool, Wai O Tapu Thermal Wonderland.jpg" by Vishal Makwana · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Champagne_Pool,_Wai_O_Tapu_Thermal_Wonderland.jpg -->

entries.name: Aguas Calientes (Colca Canyon)
entries.desc: Thermal baths near Chivay at the head of Peru's Colca Canyon, one of the deepest canyons in the world. The pools are a popular stop for travellers heading to see Andean condors in flight.

photo.alt: The terraced Colca Canyon valley near Chivay, Arequipa
photo.credit: <!-- photo: colca-canyon-valley.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Valle del Colca, Perú, 2015-08-02, DD 64.JPG" by Diego Delso · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Valle_del_Colca,_Per%C3%BA,_2015-08-02,_DD_64.JPG -->

entries.name: Soufrière Sulphur Springs
entries.desc: Billed as the Caribbean's only drive-in volcano, a geothermal field near Soufrière with bubbling sulphur pools. Visitors bathe in warm mineral and mud pools fed by the dormant volcano's activity.

photo.alt: The twin Pitons rising above Soufrière, Saint Lucia
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pitons-soufriere-saint-lucia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "The Pitons at Soufriere Saint Lucia.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_Pitons_at_Soufriere_Saint_Lucia.jpg -->

entries.name: Spa Land Centum City
entries.desc: A large modern jjimjilbang inside a shopping complex in Busan, drawing on two natural hot spring sources. It combines many mineral baths with themed dry saunas in the Korean bathhouse tradition.

photo.alt: The Centum City skyline in Busan at night
photo.credit: <!-- photo: centum-city-skyline-busan.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Busan Cinema Center and Centum City Skyline at Night.jpg" by Raja Syazwina RS · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Busan_Cinema_Center_and_Centum_City_Skyline_at_Night.jpg -->

entries.name: Leukerbad Thermal Baths
entries.desc: The largest thermal spa resort in the Alps, set in a high valley in the Valais and fed by springs used since Roman times. Its many pools sit beneath sheer cliffs at over 1,400 metres.

photo.alt: The Burgerbad thermal pools at Leukerbad in the Valais Alps
photo.credit: <!-- photo: burgerbad-thermal-baths-leukerbad.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Burgerbad thermal baths, Leukerbad.jpg" by Jess & Peter · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Burgerbad_thermal_baths,_Leukerbad.jpg -->

entries.name: Beitou Hot Springs
entries.desc: A hot spring district in the hills of Taipei, developed as a spa resort during Japanese rule. Its Thermal Valley steams with rare green sulphur water, and public and private baths line the area.

photo.alt: Steam rising from the green sulphur water of Beitou's Thermal Valley (Hell Valley)
photo.credit: <!-- photo: beitou-thermal-valley.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Beitou Hell Valley 3.JPG" by Abasaa · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Beitou_Hell_Valley_3.JPG -->

entries.name: Pai Hot Springs
entries.desc: Natural hot springs in the forested hills near the town of Pai in northern Thailand, where a hot stream cascades through a national park. The water is hot enough at the source to cook eggs.

photo.alt: A forested natural hot spring pool near Pai, northern Thailand
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sai-ngam-hot-spring-pai.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Sai Ngam Hot Spring 1.jpg" by Christophe95 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sai_Ngam_Hot_Spring_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Pamukkale Thermal Pools
entries.desc: A UNESCO World Heritage site whose name means "cotton castle," formed by mineral-laden water depositing white travertine terraces down a hillside. The Roman city of Hierapolis sits above it.

photo.alt: The white travertine terraces of Pamukkale above the Hierapolis ruins
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pamukkale-travertine-terraces.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Pamukkale Hierapolis Travertine pools.JPG" by Pvasiliadis · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pamukkale_Hierapolis_Travertine_pools.JPG -->

entries.name: Bath Thermae Spa (Roman Baths)
entries.desc: Britain's only natural hot springs, used for bathing since Roman times and giving the city of Bath its name. The modern Thermae spa lets visitors soak in the same mineral water beside the ancient site.

photo.alt: The Great Bath at the Roman Baths in Bath, England, with steaming mineral water and surrounding Roman columns
photo.credit: <!-- photo: roman-baths-great-bath-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "Roman Baths in Bath Spa, England - July 2006.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Roman_Baths_in_Bath_Spa,_England_-_July_2006.jpg -->

entries.name: Chena Hot Springs
entries.desc: A remote resort east of Fairbanks centred on an outdoor mineral rock pool. Its far-northern location makes it a prime place to soak while watching the aurora borealis in the dark winter months.

photo.alt: The outdoor rock pool at Chena Hot Springs Resort near Fairbanks, Alaska
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chena-hot-springs-outdoor-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Outdoor pool Chena Hot Springs.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Outdoor_pool_Chena_Hot_Springs.jpg -->

entries.name: Hot Springs National Park
entries.desc: A national park in Arkansas built around thermal springs and the historic Bathhouse Row, a strip of early 20th-century bathhouses. It is one of the oldest federally protected areas in the country.

photo.alt: Bathhouse Row's early 20th-century bathhouses at Hot Springs National Park, Arkansas
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bathhouse-row-hot-springs-national-park.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Bathhouse Row, Lamar, Hot Springs National Park, Arkansas.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bathhouse_Row,_Lamar,_Hot_Springs_National_Park,_Arkansas.jpg -->

entries.name: Glenwood Hot Springs
entries.desc: Home to one of the world's largest hot springs pools, on the Colorado River in the Rocky Mountains. The resort has drawn bathers since the 1880s and was long a fashionable mountain retreat.

photo.alt: The Glenwood Hot Springs pool on the Colorado River in Glenwood Springs, Colorado
photo.credit: <!-- photo: glenwood-hot-springs-pool.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Glenwood Hot Springs Pool 2020.JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Glenwood_Hot_Springs_Pool_2020.JPG -->

entries.name: Ojo Caliente
entries.tag: Ojo Caliente, New Mexico, United States
entries.desc: One of the oldest health resorts in the United States, in northern New Mexico. It is unusual for offering several distinct mineral waters, including rare lithia and iron springs, at a single site.

photo.alt: Ojo Caliente Mineral Springs Resort in northern New Mexico
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ojo-caliente-resort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Ojo Caliente mineral springs and resort (14500688944).jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ojo_Caliente_mineral_springs_and_resort_(14500688944).jpg -->

entries.name: Boiling River
entries.desc: A spot in northern Yellowstone where a large hot spring pours into the cold Gardner River, creating a soakable mix along the bank. Access depends on river levels and park conditions.

photo.alt: The Gardner River valley in northern Yellowstone National Park, where Boiling River enters the cold river
photo.credit: <!-- photo: gardner-river-valley-yellowstone.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Gardner River Valley Yellowstone NP.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gardner_River_Valley_Yellowstone_NP.jpg -->

entries.name: Grand Prismatic Spring
entries.desc: The largest hot spring in the United States and among the biggest in the world, in Yellowstone's Midway Geyser Basin. Its rainbow rings come from heat-loving microbes; it is far too hot to enter.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Grand Prismatic Spring's rainbow rings in Yellowstone's Midway Geyser Basin
photo.credit: <!-- photo: grand-prismatic-spring-aerial.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Aerial image of Grand Prismatic Spring (view from the southeast).jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aerial_image_of_Grand_Prismatic_Spring_(view_from_the_southeast).jpg -->

meta: Largest Hot Spring in the US
